title: Song of Solomon 8
subtitle: Love as Strong as Death

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “Set Me as a Seal upon Your Heart”

heb.paragraph: "Set me as a seal (ḥōṭām) upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm" (v.6). A seal was pressed into hot wax to mark ownership and identity. It was worn on a cord over the heart or as a ring on the hand. The beloved asks to be imprinted on the lover permanently — on his heart (affection) and his arm (action). Love as identity-mark.

"Love is as strong as death (ʿazzâ kammāwet), its jealousy unyielding as the grave. It burns like blazing fire, like a mighty flame" (v.6). The poem’s theological summit. Love matches death in power. The two forces that govern human existence — love and death — are equal. Neither yields. And love’s fire is literally "the flame of Yah" (shalhebet-yâ) — the only occurrence of God’s name in the poem. Love’s fire is divine fire.

refs.note: "Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Neither death nor life..." — Paul’s declaration that love defeats even death.
"Love never fails" — the permanence the Song asserts.
"Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one’s life for one’s friends" — the love stronger than death enacted at Calvary.

notes.note: "Do not arouse or awaken love until it so desires." The refrain’s third and final occurrence. The poem’s structural markers: 2:7, 3:5, 8:4.

"Set me as a seal upon your heart... for love is as strong as death, its jealousy unyielding as the grave. It burns like blazing fire, like a mighty flame." The book’s theological peak. Love equals death in power. The flame is shalhebet-yâ — "the flame of the LORD" — the only reference to God’s name in the entire poem.

"Many waters cannot quench love; rivers cannot sweep it away. If one were to give all the wealth of one’s house for love, it would be utterly scorned." Love is unquenchable and unpurchasable. It transcends both nature and commerce.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "strong as death" — the only force in human experience as absolute as love is death. Both are unavoidable, irreversible, and total in their claim. The Song asserts parity.
Alter: shalhebet-yâ — the divine name smuggled into the poem’s climax. God has been absent from the Song’s surface but present in its fire. Love’s origin is revealed at the last possible moment.
Calvin: "love is as strong as death" — Calvin: Christ’s love for the church IS stronger than death. He entered death and conquered it. The Song prophesies what Calvary accomplishes.
Calvin: love’s unquenchability is God’s own nature: "God is love" (1 John 4:8). The flame of Yah burns because God is its source. No water on earth can extinguish the divine fire.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: shalhebet-yâ is debated — "flame of Yah(weh)" or "mighty flame" (using the divine name as an intensifier). Either reading connects love’s fire to God.
The NET: "many waters" echoes the chaos-waters of creation (Gen 1:2). Even primordial chaos cannot drown love. Love predates and survives the forces of disorder.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: shalhebet-yâ — "flame of Yah" — is the Song’s only mention of God by name. It comes at the climactic moment. The fire of love is divine in origin. This single word transforms the entire poem from secular love poetry into theological statement.

Longman: "if one were to give all the wealth of one’s house for love, it would be utterly scorned" — the anti-transactional theology. Love cannot be bought. This refutes every attempt to reduce love to economics, including the friends’ transactional theology in Job.

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: the seal/death/fire/water sequence is the densest theology in the poem. Seal = permanence. Death = power. Fire = divine origin. Water = indestructibility. Four dimensions of love’s nature in four images.
Garrett: "utterly scorned" — the Hebrew is emphatic. The person who tries to buy love is not merely refused but despised. Love’s nature makes transaction obscene.

hist.context: The poem builds to its theological climax. The beloved wishes he were her brother so she could kiss him publicly without shame (vv.1–2) — the social constraints on public affection. She brings him to her mother’s house (v.2), the refrain repeats one final time (v.4), and then the definitive statement: "Set me as a seal... love is as strong as death... many waters cannot quench love; rivers cannot sweep it away" (vv.6–7). If a man offered all his wealth for love, it would be utterly scorned. Love is not purchasable, not earnable, not resistible. It is as strong as death and comes from God.

heb.paragraph: "Make haste (bəraḥ), my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the spice-laden mountains" (v.14). The poem’s final words are an invitation: come quickly. The poem ends not with resolution but with longing. Love is never finished; desire is never fully satisfied. The final image is the beloved running toward her, fleet as a deer on fragrant mountains. Love in motion, always arriving, never complete.

refs.note: "The Spirit and the bride say, ‘Come!’" — the cosmic invitation that echoes the Song’s closing "make haste."
"Come, Lord Jesus" — the final prayer of Scripture, an echo of the bride’s final cry.
"My beloved had a vineyard" — the vineyard as love/covenant metaphor across Scripture.

notes.note: "We have a little sister, and her breasts are not yet grown." Protection of the immature: if she is a wall (strong), adorn her; if she is a door (vulnerable), protect her. The community cares for those not yet ready for love.

"Solomon had a vineyard... my own vineyard is mine to give." The beloved asserts ownership of her own sexuality. It is hers to give, not Solomon’s to take. Agency is the final theological statement about love.
"Let me hear your voice." "Make haste, my beloved." The poem ends with mutual invitation. Love is never finished. The final word is not "the end" but "come."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "my own vineyard is mine" echoes and corrects 1:6. The vineyard she failed to tend is now fully possessed. The poem’s last self-reference is ownership: she belongs to herself before she gives herself.
Alter: the gazelle and young stag on the spice-laden mountains return from 2:8–9. The poem closes where it opened: with the beloved leaping toward her. The circle is both complete and open.
Calvin: "my own vineyard is mine to give" is the theology of grace applied to human love. What is freely given has infinite value; what is compelled or purchased has none.
Calvin: "make haste, my beloved" — the church’s cry to Christ: "Come, Lord Jesus" (Rev 22:20). The Song ends as Scripture ends: with longing for the Bridegroom’s return.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the vineyard contrast is economic: Solomon’s vineyard generates income (1000 shekels); hers generates love. The values are incommensurable.
The NET: the poem’s final word is an imperative of desire: come! hurry! The open ending leaves the reader in the position of the beloved: waiting, longing, calling.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: "my own vineyard is mine to give" recalls 1:6 where she neglected her own vineyard. The poem’s arc: from neglected vineyard to owned vineyard. She has claimed her own life, her own love, her own agency.

Longman: the poem ends with desire, not consummation. The beloved calls: come quickly. The open ending mirrors life: love is always in process, always calling, never complete until the final consummation of all things (Rev 22:17).

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: the vineyard contrast — Solomon’s commercial vineyard vs the beloved’s personal vineyard — asserts that love transcends economics. Her vineyard is priceless because it is freely given, not leased.
Garrett: the open ending is the Song’s most sophisticated literary choice. No resolution, no tidy conclusion. Love continues. The reader is left with longing — which is love’s natural state.

hist.context: The poem’s closing section addresses practical matters (vv.8–10: the little sister who has no breasts — protection of the immature), recaps the vineyard imagery (vv.11–12: Solomon had a vineyard; the beloved’s vineyard is her own to give), and ends with the lovers calling to each other (vv.13–14). "Let me hear your voice" (v.13). "Make haste, my beloved" (v.14). The poem closes as it opened: with desire, longing, and invitation. Love is not a destination but a journey, always calling, always running.

ppl.name: Beloved
ppl.text: Beloved is a key figure in Love as Strong as Death.

ppl.name: Lover
ppl.text: Lover is a key figure in Love as Strong as Death.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: It is hers to give, not Solomon’s to take…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIf she is a wall, we will build towers of silver on her. If she is a door, we will enclose her with panels of cedar.ESVIf she is a wall, we will build on her a battlement of silver, but if she is a door, we will enclose her with boards of cedar. SheNIVIf only you were to me like a brother, who was nursed at my mother’s breasts! Then, if I found you outside, I would kiss you, and no one would despise me.ESVOh that you were like a brother to me who nursed at my mother’s breasts! If I found you outside, I would kiss you, and none would despise me.

rec.text: ""Do not arouse or awaken love until it so desires." The refrain’s third and final occurrence. The poem’s structural markers: 2:7, 3:5, 8:4."

rows.label: Make Haste — 8:8–14
rows.text: "My vineyard is mine to give." The poem ends with longing: "Come quickly, my beloved."

It was worn on a cord over the heart or as a ring on the hand. The beloved asks to be imprinted on the lover permanently — on his heart (affection) and his arm (action). Love as identity-mark.

thread.text: "Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Neither death nor life..." — Paul’s declaration that love defeats even death.
"Love never fails" — the permanence the Song asserts.
"Greater love has no one than this: to lay down one’s life for one’s friends" — the love stronger than death enacted at Calvary.
"The Spirit and the bride say, ‘Come!’" — the cosmic invitation that echoes the Song’s closing "make haste."
"Come, Lord Jesus" — the final prayer of Scripture, an echo of the bride’s final cry.
"My beloved had a vineyard" — the vineyard as love/covenant metaphor across Scripture.

themes.note: Covenant and Prophecy both peak at 9: "love strong as death" + "flame of Yah" is the theological summit. Mercy at 8: love unpurchasable, unquenchable. Worship: the divine fire. "Come, Lord Jesus" echoes.

prayer_prompt: Love is as strong as death, its jealousy unyielding as the grave. Consider asking God about the strength of His love for you — a love that many waters cannot quench.

coaching.tip: 'Set me as a seal upon your heart' (v. 6) — the poem's theological climax. 'Love is as strong as death, jealousy unyielding as the grave. Many waters cannot quench love.' These are the strongest claims in the book: love rivals death's power. Whether human love or divine, the Song insists love is unquenchable.

The closing dialogue returns to vineyard imagery (vv. 11-12) and ends with urgency: 'Make haste, my beloved' (v. 14). The book ends not with resolution but longing. Jewish tradition placed Song of Solomon before Ruth at Passover — love poetry leading to redemption narrative. The Song's desire points beyond itself.
